content.problemStatement:
Petya is preparing a problem for a local contest in his school. The problem is to find a longest increasing subsequence in a given permutation. A permutation of size nnn is a sequence of nnn numbers a1,…,ana_1, \ldots, a_na1​,…,an​ such that every number from 111 to nnn occurs in the sequence exactly once. An increasing subsequence of length kkk of the sequence a1,…,ana_1, \ldots, a_na1​,…,an​ is a sequence of indices i1,…,iki_1, \ldots, i_ki1​,…,ik​ such that 1≤i1<…<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < \ldots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<…<ik​≤n and ai1<…<aika_{i_1} < \ldots < a_{i_k}ai1​​<…<aik​​. A longest increasing subsequence is an increasing subsequences with the largest length. Note that in general there may be several longest increasing subsequences.

Petya had some tests prepared, but then lost the input part for some of them. He now has a test for a certain value of nnn, and a sequence i1,…,iki_1, \ldots, i_ki1​,…,ik​ that is supposed to be a longest increasing subsequence. Petya now has to reconstruct a permutation of size nnn with this sequence being an answer. Petya doesn't want to take any risks, so he additionally wants this sequence to be the **only** longest increasing subsequence, that is, all other increasing subsequences have to have **strictly smaller** length. Help Petya determine if this is possible, and if so, construct any such permutation.

###Input:

The first line contains an integer TTT, denoting number of test cases.

The first line of every test case contains two integers nnn and k−k-k−  the size of the permutation and the length of the longest increasing subsequence (1≤k≤n≤1051 \leq k \leq n \leq 10^51≤k≤n≤105).

The second line contains kkk integers i1,…,ik−i_1, \ldots, i_k-i1​,…,ik​− the longest increasing subsequence (1≤i1<…<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < \ldots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<…<ik​≤n).

###Output:
If there is no permutation with the sequence i1,…,iki_1, \ldots, i_ki1​,…,ik​ being the only longest increasing subsequence, print `NO` . Otherwise, print `YES` on the first line, followed by nnn numbers describing any suitable permutation on the second line.

###Constraints


1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤k≤n≤1051 \leq k \leq n \leq 10^51≤k≤n≤105
1≤i1<…<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < \ldots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<…<ik​≤n
###Sample Input:

`2
3 2
1 2
2 1
1
`###Sample Output:

`YES
2 3 1
NO
`
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, denoting the number of test cases. The first line of every test case contains two integers n and k, the size of the permutation and the length of the longest increasing subsequence. The second line contains k integers i1, …, ik, the longest increasing subsequence.
content.outputFormat: If there is no permutation with the sequence i1, …, ik being the only longest increasing subsequence, print 'NO'. Otherwise, print 'YES' on the first line, followed by n numbers describing any suitable permutation on the second line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we can construct a permutation where the subsequence 1, 2 is the only longest increasing subsequence. For the second test case, it is not possible to construct such a permutation.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to find the longest increasing subsequence. We will then use this subsequence to construct a permutation where this subsequence is the only longest increasing subsequence. We will start by initializing an array to store the permutation. We will then fill in the permutation based on the given subsequence, making sure that the subsequence is the only longest increasing subsequence.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) — we iterate over the permutation once to fill it in
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — we need to store the permutation

code.Python3:
import sys
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    n, k = map(int, input().split())
    i = list(map(int, input().split()))
    p = [0] * n
    val = n
    for j in range(k - 1, -1, -1):
        p[i[j] - 1] = val
        val -= 1
    for j in range(n):
        if p[j] == 0:
            p[j] = val
            val -= 1
    print('YES')
    print(*p)
